cloud.title: Linux & Networking Foundations
cloud.description: Cloud engineering starts with Linux and networking fluency. Everything you deploy runs on Linux. You need to navigate servers, understand networking primitives, and automate with shell scripts.
topics: Linux file system, permissions, users & groups
Shell scripting — variables, loops, functions, cron
Networking — IP addressing, subnets, CIDR, routing
SSH — key-based auth, tunneling, bastion hosts
cloud.challenge: Provision an Ubuntu VPS (AWS free tier, DigitalOcean, or similar), set up a non-root user with SSH key auth, configure ufw firewall, and deploy a simple Node.js app as a systemd service.

cloud.title: AWS Core Services
cloud.description: AWS is the dominant cloud platform. Learn the foundational services — compute, storage, networking, and databases — that underpin almost every production architecture.
topics: IAM — users, roles, policies, least-privilege principle
VPC — subnets, security groups, NACLs, internet gateways
EC2 — instance types, AMIs, key pairs, user data scripts
S3 — buckets, objects, policies, versioning, lifecycle rules

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — AWS Full Course
A Cloud Guru — AWS Solutions Architect

cloud.challenge: Deploy a full-stack app on AWS — EC2 with Nginx, RDS PostgreSQL, S3 for static assets, proper IAM roles (no root credentials), and a VPC with public/private subnets.

cloud.title: Infrastructure as Code — Terraform
cloud.description: Stop clicking around in the console. Define your infrastructure in code, version it with Git, and deploy reproducible environments with Terraform.
topics: HCL syntax — blocks, arguments, expressions, locals
Core workflow — init, plan, apply, destroy
Remote state with S3 + DynamoDB locking

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — Terraform Full Course

cloud.challenge: Rewrite your AWS infrastructure from the previous challenge in Terraform — VPC, EC2, RDS, S3, IAM, remote state — all reproducible from a single terraform apply.

cloud.title: Containers with Docker
cloud.description: Containers are the fundamental unit of modern cloud deployment. Master Docker — from writing efficient Dockerfiles to orchestrating multi-service applications.
topics: Container concepts — images, layers, registry, runtime
Dockerfile best practices — multi-stage builds, layer caching
Docker Compose for local multi-service development
AWS ECR — push and pull private images
Container security — rootless, read-only FS, secrets
cloud.challenge: Containerize a multi-service app (frontend + API + database), push to ECR, and update your Terraform to deploy the container on EC2 pulling from ECR.

cloud.title: Kubernetes
cloud.description: Kubernetes is the industry standard for container orchestration at scale. Learn to deploy, scale, and manage containerized workloads on managed clusters.
topics: K8s architecture — control plane, nodes, pods, services
AWS EKS — managed cluster setup, IAM integration

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — Kubernetes Full Course

cloud.challenge: Deploy your containerized app to EKS — write Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, Ingress), package with Helm, and configure autoscaling based on CPU.

cloud.title: CI/CD Pipelines
cloud.description: Automate your path from code commit to production deployment. Build pipelines that test, build, scan, and deploy automatically, with GitOps principles for Kubernetes.
topics: GitHub Actions — workflows, jobs, steps, secrets, matrix builds
CI pipeline — lint, test, build Docker image, push to ECR
CD with ArgoCD — GitOps, app sync, health checks
Terraform in CI — plan on PR, apply on merge
Secrets management — GitHub Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — CI/CD Full Course

cloud.challenge: Build an end-to-end GitOps pipeline — on push: run tests, build and push Docker image to ECR, update Helm values, and let ArgoCD auto-deploy to EKS.

cloud.title: Monitoring & Observability
cloud.description: You can't fix what you can't see. Build observability into your stack with metrics, logs, and traces. Know when something breaks before your users tell you.
topics: Three pillars of observability — metrics, logs, traces
cloud.challenge: Instrument your Kubernetes workload with Prometheus metrics, build a Grafana dashboard showing request rate, error rate, and latency, and set up an alert for high error rates.

cloud.title: Security & Cost Optimization
cloud.description: Security and cost are the two areas that separate good cloud engineers from great ones. Apply defense-in-depth and understand the cloud billing model to avoid expensive surprises.
topics: IAM — least privilege, permission boundaries, service accounts
HashiCorp Vault — secrets engine, dynamic credentials
Network security — private subnets, VPN, WAF
Image scanning — Trivy, ECR native scanning
AWS Cost Explorer — reserved instances, savings plans
cloud.challenge: Audit your AWS setup against the Well-Architected Framework — fix IAM over-permissions, add Vault for DB credentials, set up billing alerts, and migrate non-critical workloads to Spot.

cloud.title: Multi-Region Architecture and Disaster Recovery
cloud.description: Design globally distributed systems on AWS that survive regional failures using active-active and active-passive topologies backed by automated failover. Apply Terraform to codify multi-region infrastructure and implement RTO/RPO targets through tested DR runbooks.
topics: Route 53 latency and failover routing policies
Aurora Global Database and cross-region read replicas
S3 Cross-Region Replication and object consistency
Global Accelerator for anycast traffic routing
Chaos and DR drills with AWS Fault Injection Simulator
RTO and RPO target definition and measurement

resources.label: Cloud Architecture Patterns by Bill Wilder

cloud.challenge: Deploy a stateless API behind Global Accelerator across two AWS regions with Aurora Global Database, automate regional failover with Route 53 health checks, and run a simulated regional outage to validate your RTO target.

cloud.title: FinOps, Platform Engineering and SRE Practices
cloud.description: Shift from infrastructure provisioning to building internal developer platforms that give teams self-service access to golden-path templates, cost visibility, and reliability tooling. Apply SRE principles including toil reduction, capacity planning, and error budget policies.
topics: FinOps framework and cloud cost allocation tagging strategies
AWS Cost Explorer, Budgets, and Savings Plans optimization
Internal developer platforms and Backstage scaffolding
Golden-path templates and platform-team operating models
Toil measurement and automation priority frameworks
Error budget policies and SRE team topologies
cloud.challenge: Set up a Backstage instance with one software template that provisions a Terraform-managed S3-backed microservice, tag all resources for cost allocation, and create a monthly budget alert tied to the project team.

cicd.title: CI/CD Foundations
cicd.description: This layer introduces the core ideas behind continuous integration and continuous delivery. It matters because every modern delivery pipeline builds on these foundational concepts and version control habits.
topics: What CI and CD mean and how they differ

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana CI/CD Crash Course

cicd.challenge: Create a repository with a single GitHub Actions workflow that runs a linter on every push.

cicd.title: Build and Test Automation
cicd.description: This layer focuses on automating builds and running test suites inside pipelines. It matters because reliable automated testing is what makes continuous integration trustworthy.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitHub Actions Tutorial

cicd.challenge: Extend the workflow to build a Docker image and run a test matrix across multiple language versions.

cicd.title: Pipeline as Code with Jenkins
cicd.description: This layer covers declarative and scripted pipelines using Jenkins. It matters because many enterprises run Jenkins and pipeline as code keeps delivery reproducible.
cicd.challenge: Recreate your build and test pipeline as a Jenkinsfile running on a containerized agent.

cicd.title: Artifact Management and Versioning
cicd.description: This layer teaches how to publish, version, and store build outputs. It matters because traceable artifacts are essential for reliable releases and rollbacks.
cicd.challenge: Add a job that builds, versions with semantic tags, and pushes the image to a registry on merges to main.

cicd.title: Deployment Strategies
cicd.description: This layer explores how to ship code safely to environments. It matters because the deployment strategy directly controls user-facing risk during releases.
cicd.challenge: Implement a canary deployment job that promotes to production only after a manual approval gate.

cicd.title: GitOps with ArgoCD
cicd.description: This layer introduces declarative continuous delivery driven by Git as the source of truth. It matters because GitOps makes deployments auditable, reversible, and self-healing.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitOps with ArgoCD

cicd.challenge: Set up ArgoCD to continuously sync your application from a Git repository to a cluster.

cicd.title: Secrets and Security in Pipelines
cicd.description: This layer covers securely handling credentials and signing artifacts in delivery pipelines. It matters because pipelines are a prime target for supply chain attacks.
cicd.challenge: Replace static cloud credentials with OIDC federation and add an image scan gate to the pipeline.

cicd.title: Pipeline Observability and Metrics
cicd.description: This layer teaches how to measure and visualize pipeline health and delivery performance. It matters because you cannot improve delivery speed without measuring it.
cicd.challenge: Export pipeline run metrics and build a Grafana dashboard showing deployment frequency and lead time.

cicd.title: Scaling and Reusable Pipelines
cicd.description: This layer focuses on sharing and standardizing pipelines across many repositories. It matters because consistency at scale reduces maintenance and enforces best practices.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana CI/CD at Scale

cicd.challenge: Refactor your pipeline into a reusable workflow consumed by two separate service repositories.

cicd.title: Production Delivery Operations
cicd.description: This layer covers operating delivery systems reliably in production at organizational scale. It matters because a delivery platform must stay available, secure, and fast under real load.
cicd.challenge: Operate an end-to-end GitOps delivery platform with progressive rollouts and automated rollback on failure.

sre.title: SRE Fundamentals
sre.description: This layer introduces the principles and mindset of site reliability engineering. It matters because reliability work is grounded in shared definitions of risk, toil, and service objectives.
topics: What SRE is and how it relates to DevOps
sre.challenge: Write a one-page reliability charter defining SLIs and an error budget for a sample service.

sre.title: Metrics and Monitoring with Prometheus
sre.description: This layer teaches how to collect and query time series metrics. It matters because metrics are the foundation of understanding system behavior and health.
sre.challenge: Instrument a Go service with custom metrics and scrape them with a local Prometheus instance.

sre.title: Dashboards and Visualization with Grafana
sre.description: This layer covers turning metrics into actionable dashboards. It matters because clear visualization is how teams spot trends and diagnose problems quickly.
sre.challenge: Build a Grafana dashboard that tracks the latency and error rate of your Go service.

sre.title: Service Level Objectives
sre.description: This layer focuses on defining and measuring SLOs and error budgets. It matters because SLOs translate reliability goals into concrete, enforceable targets.
sre.challenge: Define an SLO for your service and create burn-rate alerts that fire when the error budget depletes too fast.

sre.title: Alerting and On-Call with PagerDuty
sre.description: This layer teaches how to route alerts and run sustainable on-call rotations. It matters because well-designed alerting protects both systems and the humans who operate them.
sre.challenge: Wire Alertmanager to PagerDuty so your burn-rate alerts page an on-call schedule with escalation.

sre.title: Logging and Distributed Tracing
sre.description: This layer covers collecting logs and traces to understand request flows. It matters because metrics alone cannot explain why a specific request failed.
sre.challenge: Add OpenTelemetry tracing to your Go service and correlate a slow trace with its logs and metrics.

sre.title: Incident Management
sre.description: This layer focuses on responding to and learning from production incidents. It matters because how an organization handles incidents shapes its long-term reliability.
sre.challenge: Run a simulated incident using a runbook and write a blameless postmortem with action items.

sre.title: Reliability Engineering in Go
sre.description: This layer teaches building resilient services and tooling in Go. It matters because SREs often write automation and reliability features directly into services.
sre.challenge: Add timeouts, retries with backoff, and a circuit breaker to your Go service and expose their metrics.

sre.title: Capacity Planning and Chaos Engineering
sre.description: This layer covers forecasting demand and proactively testing failure modes. It matters because resilient systems are validated under stress rather than assumed.
sre.challenge: Run a chaos experiment that injects latency and verify your SLOs and alerts behave as expected.

sre.title: Operating Reliability at Scale
sre.description: This layer covers running observability and reliability practices across a large production fleet. It matters because reliability programs must scale with the organization without collapsing under toil.
sre.challenge: Stand up a federated, long-term observability stack and produce a reliability scorecard for multiple services.

infra.title: Linux and Infrastructure Basics
infra.description: This layer builds the operating system and networking foundation every infrastructure engineer needs. It matters because infrastructure tooling ultimately manages Linux hosts, networks, and processes.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana Linux Crash Course

infra.challenge: Write a Bash script that provisions and configures a service on a fresh Linux virtual machine.

infra.title: Infrastructure as Code Concepts
infra.description: This layer introduces the principles of declaring infrastructure in version-controlled code. It matters because manual infrastructure changes are error-prone and impossible to reproduce reliably.
infra.challenge: Use Terraform to declare and create a basic virtual network and compute instance from code.

infra.title: Provisioning with Terraform
infra.description: This layer deepens Terraform usage for real provisioning workflows. It matters because Terraform is the dominant tool for cloud infrastructure provisioning.
infra.challenge: Parameterize your Terraform configuration with variables and outputs to deploy to two environments.

infra.title: Configuration Management with Ansible
infra.description: This layer covers automating server configuration after provisioning. It matters because consistent configuration prevents snowflake servers and configuration drift.
infra.challenge: Write an Ansible playbook that configures and hardens the servers Terraform provisioned.

infra.title: Reusable Modules and Roles
infra.description: This layer teaches structuring infrastructure code for reuse and maintainability. It matters because modular code scales across teams and reduces duplication.
infra.challenge: Refactor your Terraform and Ansible code into reusable modules and roles consumed by multiple stacks.

infra.title: Programmatic IaC with Pulumi
infra.description: This layer introduces defining infrastructure using general-purpose programming languages. It matters because programmatic IaC unlocks loops, conditionals, and richer abstractions.
infra.challenge: Reimplement one of your Terraform stacks in Pulumi using a real programming language.

infra.title: State, Secrets, and Remote Backends
infra.description: This layer covers managing infrastructure state and sensitive values safely. It matters because corrupted state or leaked secrets can cause outages and breaches.
infra.challenge: Migrate Terraform state to a remote locking backend and inject secrets from Vault at apply time.

infra.title: Infrastructure CI/CD and Testing
infra.description: This layer teaches automating and validating infrastructure changes in pipelines. It matters because applying infrastructure by hand at scale is slow and risky.
infra.challenge: Build a pipeline that lints, plans, tests, and applies your Terraform with a manual approval gate.

infra.title: Multi-Environment and Multi-Cloud
infra.description: This layer covers structuring infrastructure across environments and providers. It matters because real organizations run separate environments and sometimes multiple clouds.
infra.challenge: Lay out dev, staging, and prod environments from shared modules with isolated state per environment.

infra.title: Operating Infrastructure in Production
infra.description: This layer covers running, scaling, and recovering infrastructure reliably in production. It matters because infrastructure must remain stable, recoverable, and compliant under real workloads.
infra.challenge: Operate your full IaC stack with automated drift detection, backups, and a documented recovery runbook.

kubernetes.title: Containers and Docker Fundamentals
kubernetes.description: This layer establishes the container fundamentals that Kubernetes builds upon. It matters because you cannot operate Kubernetes well without understanding images, containers, and isolation.
kubernetes.challenge: Containerize a small web application and run it with a database using Docker Compose.

kubernetes.title: Kubernetes Core Concepts
kubernetes.description: This layer introduces the Kubernetes architecture and core objects. It matters because pods, services, and the control plane are the vocabulary of all later work.
kubernetes.challenge: Deploy your containerized app to a local cluster using Deployments and expose it with a Service.

kubernetes.title: Configuration and Storage
kubernetes.description: This layer covers managing application configuration and persistent data. It matters because real applications need configuration, secrets, and durable storage.
kubernetes.challenge: Run a stateful database in the cluster using a StatefulSet with persistent storage and Secrets.

kubernetes.title: Packaging with Helm
kubernetes.description: This layer teaches templating and packaging Kubernetes applications with Helm. It matters because Helm makes complex deployments repeatable and configurable.
kubernetes.challenge: Package your application as a Helm chart with configurable values for multiple environments.

kubernetes.title: Networking and Ingress
kubernetes.description: This layer explores how traffic flows in and across a cluster. It matters because exposing and securing services correctly is central to running real workloads.
kubernetes.challenge: Expose multiple services through an Ingress controller with TLS and path-based routing.

kubernetes.title: Service Mesh with Istio
kubernetes.description: This layer introduces advanced traffic management and security with a service mesh. It matters because meshes provide observability, mTLS, and traffic control without code changes.
kubernetes.challenge: Install Istio and use traffic splitting to roll out a canary version of one of your services.

kubernetes.title: Security and RBAC
kubernetes.description: This layer covers securing clusters and workloads. It matters because misconfigured permissions and pods are a leading cause of cluster breaches.
kubernetes.challenge: Lock down a namespace with RBAC, pod security standards, and an admission policy that blocks risky pods.

kubernetes.title: Observability and Autoscaling
kubernetes.description: This layer teaches monitoring clusters and scaling workloads automatically. It matters because clusters must adapt to load while remaining observable and cost-efficient.
kubernetes.challenge: Add resource limits, a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, and Prometheus monitoring to your workloads.

kubernetes.title: GitOps and Cluster Delivery
kubernetes.description: This layer covers deploying to clusters declaratively from Git. It matters because GitOps makes cluster changes auditable, repeatable, and self-healing.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitOps with ArgoCD

kubernetes.challenge: Deliver your Helm-packaged application to the cluster through ArgoCD synced from Git.

kubernetes.title: Production Cluster Operations
kubernetes.description: This layer covers operating clusters reliably at production scale. It matters because production clusters must stay available, upgradeable, and recoverable under real traffic.
kubernetes.challenge: Operate a production-grade cluster with backups, an upgrade plan, quotas, and a documented recovery runbook.

aws-dev.title: AWS Cloud Fundamentals
aws-dev.description: This layer introduces the AWS global infrastructure and core account concepts. It matters because regions, identity, and networking underpin every service you will use.
aws-dev.challenge: Create an IAM user with least-privilege policies and access AWS through both the console and CLI.

aws-dev.title: Compute with EC2
aws-dev.description: This layer covers provisioning and managing virtual servers on AWS. It matters because EC2 remains a core compute primitive and teaches fundamental cloud operations.
aws-dev.challenge: Launch an EC2 instance behind a load balancer in an Auto Scaling group serving a simple web app.

aws-dev.title: Storage with S3
aws-dev.description: This layer teaches object storage and content delivery on AWS. It matters because S3 is the backbone of storage, backups, and static hosting in the AWS ecosystem.
aws-dev.challenge: Host a static site on S3 with versioning, lifecycle rules, and a CloudFront distribution in front.

aws-dev.title: Serverless with Lambda
aws-dev.description: This layer covers building event-driven serverless applications. It matters because serverless reduces operational overhead and is a key modern AWS pattern.
aws-dev.challenge: Build a serverless REST API using Lambda, API Gateway, and DynamoDB for a small data service.

aws-dev.title: Networking and Security
aws-dev.description: This layer deepens VPC networking and access control. It matters because secure, well-segmented networks are required for any production AWS workload.
aws-dev.challenge: Design a VPC with public and private subnets and place your app and database in the correct tiers.

aws-dev.title: Infrastructure as Code with CloudFormation
aws-dev.description: This layer teaches declaring AWS infrastructure as templates. It matters because reproducible, version-controlled infrastructure is essential for reliable AWS environments.
aws-dev.challenge: Reprovision your EC2, S3, and networking resources entirely through a CloudFormation template.

aws-dev.title: Databases and Messaging
aws-dev.description: This layer covers managed databases and asynchronous messaging on AWS. It matters because durable data and decoupled communication are core to scalable architectures.
aws-dev.challenge: Add an RDS database and an SQS queue to decouple a background worker in your serverless API.

aws-dev.title: Observability and Operations
aws-dev.description: This layer teaches monitoring, logging, and tracing AWS workloads. It matters because visibility into systems is required to operate them reliably.
aws-dev.challenge: Add CloudWatch alarms, dashboards, and X-Ray tracing to your application and route alerts to email.

aws-dev.title: CI/CD on AWS
aws-dev.description: This layer covers automating builds and deployments using AWS native tooling. It matters because automated delivery is what makes cloud iteration fast and safe.
aws-dev.challenge: Build a CodePipeline that tests and deploys your CloudFormation stack on every commit.

aws-dev.title: Production Architecture and Scaling
aws-dev.description: This layer covers running well-architected, scalable workloads in production. It matters because production AWS systems must balance reliability, cost, and security under real demand.
aws-dev.challenge: Architect and deploy a highly available, multi-AZ version of your application reviewed against the Well-Architected pillars.

gcp-dev.title: GCP Cloud Fundamentals
gcp-dev.description: This layer introduces the Google Cloud platform structure and identity model. It matters because projects, IAM, and networking are the foundation of every GCP service.
gcp-dev.challenge: Set up a GCP project with IAM roles and access resources through the gcloud CLI.

gcp-dev.title: Compute and Cloud Run
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers running workloads from virtual machines to serverless containers. It matters because choosing the right compute option shapes cost and operational effort.
gcp-dev.challenge: Containerize a service and deploy it to Cloud Run with autoscaling enabled.

gcp-dev.title: Storage and Databases
gcp-dev.description: This layer teaches storing objects and structured data on GCP. It matters because durable storage and the right database choice are central to most applications.
gcp-dev.challenge: Store uploaded files in Cloud Storage and persist application data in Cloud SQL or Firestore.

gcp-dev.title: Messaging with Pub/Sub
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers asynchronous, event-driven communication on GCP. It matters because decoupling services with messaging is essential for scalable systems.
gcp-dev.challenge: Wire a Pub/Sub topic that triggers a Cloud Run service to process events asynchronously.

gcp-dev.title: Networking and Security
gcp-dev.description: This layer deepens VPC networking and access control on GCP. It matters because secure, well-structured networks are required for production workloads.
gcp-dev.challenge: Place your services behind a global load balancer in a VPC with restrictive firewall rules.

gcp-dev.title: Data Analytics with BigQuery
gcp-dev.description: This layer introduces large-scale analytics and data warehousing. It matters because BigQuery enables fast analysis over huge datasets without managing infrastructure.
gcp-dev.challenge: Stream events from Pub/Sub into BigQuery and run analytical queries over the collected data.

gcp-dev.title: Kubernetes with GKE
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers running containerized workloads on managed Kubernetes. It matters because GKE is the standard way to run complex, scalable container platforms on GCP.
gcp-dev.challenge: Deploy a multi-service application to a GKE cluster with autoscaling node pools and Ingress.

gcp-dev.title: Observability and Operations
gcp-dev.description: This layer teaches monitoring, logging, and tracing GCP workloads. It matters because operating services reliably requires deep visibility into their behavior.
gcp-dev.challenge: Add monitoring dashboards, alerting policies, and tracing to your Cloud Run and GKE services.

gcp-dev.title: Infrastructure as Code and CI/CD
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers automating GCP infrastructure and deployments. It matters because reproducible infrastructure and pipelines are required for safe, fast delivery.
topics: Deploying to Cloud Run and GKE
gcp-dev.challenge: Provision your GCP resources with Terraform and deploy them via a Cloud Build pipeline on each commit.

gcp-dev.title: Production Architecture and Scaling
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers running well-architected, scalable systems in production on GCP. It matters because production systems must balance reliability, cost, and security under real load.
gcp-dev.challenge: Architect and deploy a multi-region, highly available version of your application reviewed against the architecture framework.

azure-dev.title: Azure Cloud Fundamentals
azure-dev.description: This layer introduces the Azure account structure and identity model. It matters because subscriptions, resource groups, and identity govern everything you build on Azure.
azure-dev.challenge: Create a resource group and assign RBAC roles, then manage resources through the Azure CLI.

azure-dev.title: Compute and App Services
azure-dev.description: This layer covers Azure compute options from virtual machines to serverless. It matters because selecting the right hosting model shapes scalability and operational cost.
azure-dev.challenge: Deploy a web application to App Service and add an Azure Function triggered by an HTTP request.

azure-dev.title: Storage and Databases
azure-dev.description: This layer teaches storing files and structured data on Azure. It matters because durable storage and appropriate database choices are central to most applications.
azure-dev.challenge: Store uploads in Blob Storage and persist application data in Azure SQL or Cosmos DB.

azure-dev.title: Serverless with Azure Functions
azure-dev.description: This layer covers building event-driven serverless solutions on Azure. It matters because serverless lowers operational overhead and is a core modern Azure pattern.
azure-dev.challenge: Build an event-driven workflow where a Service Bus message triggers an Azure Function to process data.

azure-dev.title: Networking and Security
azure-dev.description: This layer deepens Azure networking and access control. It matters because secure, segmented networks and managed secrets are required for production workloads.
azure-dev.challenge: Place your app behind an Application Gateway in a virtual network and load its secrets from Key Vault.

azure-dev.title: Infrastructure as Code with Bicep
azure-dev.description: This layer teaches declaring Azure infrastructure with Bicep. It matters because reproducible, version-controlled infrastructure is essential for reliable Azure environments.
azure-dev.challenge: Reprovision your compute, storage, and networking resources entirely through a Bicep template.

azure-dev.title: Kubernetes with AKS
azure-dev.description: This layer covers running containerized workloads on managed Kubernetes. It matters because AKS is the standard way to run scalable container platforms on Azure.
azure-dev.challenge: Deploy a multi-service application to an AKS cluster with autoscaling node pools and Ingress.

azure-dev.title: Observability and Operations
azure-dev.description: This layer teaches monitoring, logging, and tracing Azure workloads. It matters because visibility into systems is required to operate them reliably.
azure-dev.challenge: Add Application Insights and Azure Monitor alerts to your services with a Log Analytics dashboard.

azure-dev.title: CI/CD with Azure DevOps
azure-dev.description: This layer covers automating builds and deployments with Azure DevOps. It matters because automated delivery is what makes cloud iteration fast and reliable.
azure-dev.challenge: Build an Azure Pipeline that tests and deploys your Bicep-defined infrastructure on every commit.

azure-dev.title: Production Architecture and Scaling
azure-dev.description: This layer covers running well-architected, scalable systems in production on Azure. It matters because production systems must balance reliability, cost, and security under real load.
azure-dev.challenge: Architect and deploy a multi-region, highly available version of your application reviewed against the Well-Architected pillars.

devsecops.title: Security Fundamentals for DevOps
devsecops.description: This layer introduces core security concepts within a DevOps workflow. It matters because shifting security left starts with understanding threats, risk, and the secure development lifecycle.
devsecops.challenge: Build a threat model for a sample web application and document its top risks and mitigations.

devsecops.title: Dependency and SCA Scanning with Snyk
devsecops.description: This layer covers finding vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies. It matters because most modern applications inherit risk from open-source libraries.
devsecops.challenge: Scan a project with Snyk and remediate the highest severity dependency vulnerabilities it reports.

devsecops.title: Static Analysis with SonarQube
devsecops.description: This layer teaches scanning source code for bugs and security flaws. It matters because static analysis catches insecure code before it ever runs.
devsecops.challenge: Add a SonarQube quality gate to a pipeline that fails the build on new security hotspots.

devsecops.title: Container and Image Security with Trivy
devsecops.description: This layer covers scanning container images and configurations for vulnerabilities. It matters because containers can ship known vulnerabilities and misconfigurations into production.
devsecops.challenge: Scan your container images and IaC with Trivy and harden the base image to remove critical findings.

devsecops.title: Secrets Management with Vault
devsecops.description: This layer teaches storing and distributing secrets securely. It matters because hardcoded credentials are a frequent and dangerous source of breaches.
devsecops.challenge: Use Vault to issue dynamic database credentials and inject them into a running application.

devsecops.title: Secure CI/CD Pipelines
devsecops.description: This layer covers hardening the delivery pipeline itself. It matters because pipelines are a high-value target and a single weak step can compromise everything.
devsecops.challenge: Add SCA, SAST, and image scanning gates to a pipeline and replace static credentials with OIDC.

devsecops.title: Dynamic Testing and Runtime Security
devsecops.description: This layer covers testing running applications and detecting threats at runtime. It matters because some vulnerabilities only appear when systems are actually executing.
devsecops.challenge: Run a ZAP scan against your deployed app and configure Falco to alert on suspicious runtime behavior.

devsecops.title: Policy as Code and Compliance
devsecops.description: This layer teaches enforcing security and compliance rules automatically. It matters because manual policy enforcement does not scale and is easily bypassed.
devsecops.challenge: Write OPA policies that block non-compliant Terraform plans and insecure Kubernetes manifests.

devsecops.title: Supply Chain Security
devsecops.description: This layer covers securing the software supply chain end to end. It matters because attackers increasingly target build systems and dependencies rather than running apps.
devsecops.challenge: Sign your container images with Sigstore and enforce signature verification before deployment.

devsecops.title: Security Operations at Scale
devsecops.description: This layer covers running security operations across a production organization. It matters because security must be continuous, measurable, and integrated into daily operations at scale.
devsecops.challenge: Integrate scanning, runtime alerts, and signing into a unified security dashboard with an incident playbook.

platform-engineer.title: Platform Engineering Foundations
platform-engineer.description: This layer introduces the goals and mindset of internal developer platforms. It matters because platform engineering aims to reduce cognitive load and accelerate every developer in the organization.
topics: What an internal developer platform is
platform-engineer.challenge: Write a one-page vision for an internal platform defining its first golden path for developers.

platform-engineer.title: Kubernetes as a Platform Base
platform-engineer.description: This layer establishes Kubernetes as the runtime substrate for the platform. It matters because most internal platforms build their abstractions on top of Kubernetes.
platform-engineer.challenge: Provision a shared cluster with isolated namespaces and quotas for two sample tenant teams.

platform-engineer.title: Infrastructure as Code for Platforms
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers provisioning platform infrastructure declaratively. It matters because the platform itself must be reproducible, versioned, and reviewable.
platform-engineer.challenge: Build reusable Terraform modules that provision a standard environment for a new tenant team.

platform-engineer.title: Developer Portals with Backstage
platform-engineer.description: This layer introduces building a developer portal as the platform front door. It matters because a portal centralizes discovery, documentation, and self-service for developers.
platform-engineer.challenge: Stand up a Backstage instance and register your sample services in the software catalog.

platform-engineer.title: Golden Paths and Templates
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers building paved roads that let developers create services quickly. It matters because templated golden paths encode best practices and reduce setup friction.
platform-engineer.challenge: Create a Backstage template that scaffolds a new service with CI/CD and catalog registration built in.

platform-engineer.title: GitOps Delivery with ArgoCD
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers delivering platform and tenant workloads through GitOps. It matters because GitOps gives the platform auditable, self-healing, declarative deployments.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitOps with ArgoCD

platform-engineer.challenge: Deliver tenant applications through ArgoCD using an app of apps pattern driven from Git.

platform-engineer.title: Self-Service APIs and Operators
platform-engineer.description: This layer teaches exposing infrastructure as self-service Kubernetes APIs. It matters because custom abstractions let developers request resources without learning the underlying tools.
platform-engineer.challenge: Use Crossplane to expose a self-service API that lets developers provision a database with one manifest.

platform-engineer.title: Observability and Golden Signals
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers building observability into the platform for all tenants. It matters because a platform must give every team consistent visibility without each reinventing it.
platform-engineer.challenge: Provide a default observability stack so any service onboarded to the platform gets dashboards automatically.

platform-engineer.title: Policy, Security, and Governance
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers enforcing standards and security across the platform. It matters because platforms must keep all tenants compliant and secure without slowing them down.
platform-engineer.challenge: Add platform guardrails with OPA policies and per-tenant secrets so teams stay secure by default.

platform-engineer.title: Operating the Platform at Scale
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers running and evolving the platform as a product in production. It matters because a platform must scale to many teams while staying reliable, adopted, and continuously improved.
platform-engineer.challenge: Operate the full platform end to end, onboard multiple teams through golden paths, and measure adoption.
